title: Candy
description: Distribute candies to children based on ratings ensuring constraints

examples.explanation:
Initial candies: [1, 1, 1]
Pass 1:
  i=0: No left neighbor. Right: ratings[0]=1 > ratings[1]=0? No (1>0 is true, but candies[0]=1 <= candies[1]=1? Yes, but constraint is for higher rating to have more candies, so child 0 should have more than child 1? Actually if rating is higher, should have more candies. Here 1>0, so child 0 should have > child 1. Currently equal, so violation. Increase candies[0] to candies[1]+1=2. candies=[2,1,1], changed=True
  i=1: Left: ratings[1]=0 > ratings[0]=1? No. Right: ratings[1]=0 > ratings[2]=2? No
  i=2: Left: ratings[2]=2 > ratings[1]=0? Yes, and candies[2]=1 <= candies[1]=1? Yes, violation. Increase candies[2] to candies[1]+1=2. candies=[2,1,2], changed=True
End pass 1, changed=True, continue
Pass 2:
  i=0: Left: none. Right: ratings[0]=1 > ratings[1]=0? Yes, candies[0]=2 <= candies[1]=1? No (2>1), so OK
  i=1: Left: ratings[1]=0 > ratings[0]=1? No. Right: ratings[1]=0 > ratings[2]=2? No
  i=2: Left: ratings[2]=2 > ratings[1]=0? Yes, candies[2]=2 <= candies[1]=1? No (2>1), OK. Right: none
No changes, changed=False, exit loop
Sum candies: 2+1+2=5

Initial candies: [1, 1, 1]
Pass 1:
  i=0: No left. Right: ratings[0]=1 > ratings[1]=2? No
  i=1: Left: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[0]=1? Yes, candies[1]=1 <= candies[0]=1? Yes, violation. Increase candies[1] to candies[0]+1=2. candies=[1,2,1], changed=True
  Right: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[2]=2? No (equal ratings don't require more candies)
  i=2: Left: ratings[2]=2 > ratings[1]=2? No (equal). No right
End pass 1, changed=True
Pass 2:
  i=0: Right: ratings[0]=1 > ratings[1]=2? No
  i=1: Left: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[0]=1? Yes, candies[1]=2 <= candies[0]=1? No (2>1), OK. Right: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[2]=2? No
  i=2: Left: ratings[2]=2 > ratings[1]=2? No
No changes, exit
Sum: 1+2+1=4

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Initialize all children with 1 candy, then repeatedly scan the array adjusting candies until no child violates the rating constraints. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Candy Distribution
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution to distribute minimum candies while satisfying rating constraints

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Single Pass Violation Check and Fix
ladders.desc: Scan through children once, adjusting candies where rating constraints are violated
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine forms the core scanning operation of the brute force approach. The intuition is that by examining each child in sequence and comparing with immediate neighbors, we can identify and fix local violations. This works because the candy distribution constraints are local—each child only needs to satisfy conditions relative to immediate neighbors. The algorithm performs one linear scan through the array. For each position i, it checks two conditions: if the child has a higher rating than the left neighbor (if exists) but doesn't have more candies, we increase candies[i] to be one more than the left neighbor. Similarly for the right neighbor. We must check both directions because a child might be violating constraints on either side. The key insight is that fixing one violation might create new violations elsewhere, which is why the full solution needs multiple passes. Time complexity for this single scan is O(n) since we examine each child once. Space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the candy array. This connects to L0 as the inner loop that gets repeated until no changes occur. Common mistakes include not using temporary variables when adjusting candies (though here direct assignment is fine), forgetting boundary checks for first/last child, or incorrectly handling equal ratings (children with equal ratings can have equal candies). The real pattern involves careful index bounds checking and comparison logic.

Pseudocode:
function scanAndFix(ratings, candies):
    n = length(ratings)
    changed = False
    
    for i from 0 to n-1:
        # Check left neighbor
        if i > 0 and ratings[i] > ratings[i-1] and candies[i] <= candies[i-1]:
            candies[i] = candies[i-1] + 1
            changed = True
        # Check right neighbor
        if i < n-1 and ratings[i] > ratings[i+1] and candies[i] <= candies[i+1]:
            candies[i] = candies[i+1] + 1
            changed = True
    
    return changed

examples.trace:
Start: candies=[1,1,1]
i=0: Left neighbor? No (i=0). Right: ratings[0]=1 > ratings[1]=3? No
No change
i=1: Left: ratings[1]=3 > ratings[0]=1? Yes, and candies[1]=1 <= candies[0]=1? Yes, violation. Set candies[1]=candies[0]+1=2. candies=[1,2,1], changed=True
Right: ratings[1]=3 > ratings[2]=2? Yes, and candies[1]=2 <= candies[2]=1? No (2>1), so OK
i=2: Left: ratings[2]=2 > ratings[1]=3? No. Right neighbor? No (i=2)
End scan, changed=True

Start: candies=[2,1,1]
i=0: Left? No. Right: ratings[0]=2 > ratings[1]=1? Yes, candies[0]=2 <= candies[1]=1? No (2>1), OK
i=1: Left: ratings[1]=1 > ratings[0]=2? No. Right: ratings[1]=1 > ratings[2]=3? No
i=2: Left: ratings[2]=3 > ratings[1]=1? Yes, candies[2]=1 <= candies[1]=1? Yes, violation. Set candies[2]=candies[1]+1=2. candies=[2,1,2], changed=True
Right? No
End scan, changed=True

ladders.title: Core Logic — Local Constraint Checking and Adjustment
ladders.desc: Determine if a child violates candy constraints with neighbors and adjust accordingly
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the fundamental comparison logic at each position. The intuition is that candy distribution follows a simple local rule: if a child has a higher rating than a neighbor, they must have strictly more candies than that neighbor. This creates a directed requirement—the constraint only applies when ratings differ, not when they're equal. The algorithm for a single child examines up to two comparisons (left and right). For each neighbor direction, we check: 1) Is the current child's rating higher than the neighbor's? 2) If yes, does the current child have at most as many candies as the neighbor? If both conditions are true, we have a violation and must increase the current child's candies to be exactly one more than the neighbor's. The 'one more' ensures minimal increase while satisfying the constraint. This logic works because it directly encodes the problem's requirements. Time complexity for checking one child is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the atomic operation performed during the scan. Common mistakes include using 'greater than or equal' instead of strictly greater for rating comparison (equal ratings don't require inequality), forgetting to check if candies are 'less than or equal' rather than just 'less than', or not handling the case where multiple adjustments might be needed from different neighbors (though the algorithm handles this by taking the maximum required increase). The real pattern involves clear conditional logic with careful operator selection.

Pseudocode:
function checkAndAdjustChild(i, ratings, candies):
    changed = False
    n = length(ratings)
    
    # Check left neighbor
    if i > 0 and ratings[i] > ratings[i-1] and candies[i] <= candies[i-1]:
        candies[i] = candies[i-1] + 1
        changed = True
    
    # Check right neighbor
    if i < n-1 and ratings[i] > ratings[i+1] and candies[i] <= candies[i+1]:
        candies[i] = candies[i+1] + 1
        changed = True
    
    return changed

examples.trace:
Child at index 1 has rating 3
Check left neighbor (index 0): rating 3 > rating 1? Yes. candies[1]=1 <= candies[0]=1? Yes. Violation! Set candies[1] = candies[0] + 1 = 2. changed=True
Check right neighbor (index 2): rating 3 > rating 2? Yes. candies[1]=2 <= candies[2]=1? No (2>1), so no violation
Final: changed=True, candies=[1,2,1]

Child at index 0 has rating 2
Left neighbor? No (i=0)
Check right neighbor (index 1): rating 2 > rating 1? Yes. candies[0]=2 <= candies[1]=1? No (2>1), so no violation
changed=False, candies unchanged

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Neighbor Access
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array while safely accessing adjacent elements
ladders.explanation:
This level addresses the fundamental programming pattern of traversing a collection while checking neighboring elements. The intuition is that many array algorithms need to compare each element with its neighbors, but we must handle the boundaries (first and last elements) specially since they don't have neighbors on one side. This pattern works by looping through indices and using conditional checks before accessing i-1 or i+1 to avoid index errors. For the candy problem, we need to check both left and right neighbors, so we need two separate boundary checks: i > 0 before accessing i-1, and i < n-1 before accessing i+1. This ensures we only compare with existing neighbors. The algorithm simply iterates from 0 to n-1, performing operations that may reference ratings[i-1], ratings[i], and ratings[i+1] with proper guards. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as the structural framework that enables the constraint checking—without safe neighbor access, we couldn't implement the comparison logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, forgetting boundary checks entirely (leading to index out of bounds), or incorrectly assuming arrays are 1-indexed. The real pattern involves clean loop control with explicit edge case handling.

Pseudocode:
function traverseWithNeighbors(arr):
    n = length(arr)
    for i from 0 to n-1:
        # Access current element
        current = arr[i]
        
        # Access left neighbor if exists
        if i > 0:
            left = arr[i-1]
            # Do something with current and left
        
        # Access right neighbor if exists
        if i < n-1:
            right = arr[i+1]
            # Do something with current and right

examples.trace:
n=3
i=0: current=10
  Left neighbor? i>0? No (0>0 false), skip
  Right neighbor? i<n-1? 0<2 true, right=arr[1]=20
  Can compare current(10) with right(20)
i=1: current=20
  Left? i>0? 1>0 true, left=arr[0]=10
  Right? i<n-1? 1<2 true, right=arr[2]=30
  Can compare with both neighbors
i=2: current=30
  Left? 2>0 true, left=arr[1]=20
  Right? i<n-1? 2<2 false, skip
  Only left neighbor accessible

n=1
i=0: current=5
  Left? i>0? 0>0 false, skip
  Right? i<n-1? 0<0 false, skip
  No neighbors to access

Two elements, each has one neighbor

Three elements, middle accesses both neighbors

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison and Conditional Update
ladders.desc: Compare two values and update one based on a condition
ladders.explanation:
This level covers the fundamental programming operation of comparing two values and conditionally updating one. The intuition is simple: we often need to check if a relationship holds between two values, and if so, modify one of them. In the candy problem, this manifests as comparing ratings and candies between neighbors. The basic pattern is: if value A has a certain relationship to value B (like A > B), and another condition holds (like C ≤ D), then update C to a new value (typically D + 1). This works because it directly implements the problem's constraints. The algorithm involves straightforward comparison operators (> for ratings, ≤ for candies) and assignment. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as the core operation performed during array traversal—once we have access to neighbor values, we need to compare and potentially update. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operators (≥ instead of >, or < instead of ≤), forgetting that both conditions must be true for an update, or incorrectly calculating the new value. The real pattern is a simple if-statement with compound conditions.

Pseudocode:
# Generic pattern
if condition1 and condition2:
    update_value = reference_value + adjustment

# Candy-specific example
if rating_current > rating_neighbor and candies_current <= candies_neighbor:
    candies_current = candies_neighbor + 1

examples.trace:
Check condition1: rating_current > rating_neighbor? 5 > 3? Yes
Check condition2: candies_current <= candies_neighbor? 2 <= 4? Yes
Both conditions true, so should update candies_current to candies_neighbor + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
Wait, but in candy problem logic, if current has higher rating, it should have MORE candies than neighbor. Here current has 2, neighbor has 4, so violation. Actually yes, should update to 5. Let me correct: Both conditions true, so update candies_current to 5

Check condition1: rating_current > rating_neighbor? 2 > 4? No
Since first condition false, no update needed regardless of second condition
candies_current remains 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Initialization and Looping
ladders.desc: Create an array with default values and iterate through it
ladders.explanation:
This is the most fundamental prerequisite concept: creating an array (list) and looping through its elements. The intuition is that we need a data structure to store values for each child (their candy counts), and we need to process each child systematically. Arrays allow us to store multiple values indexed by position, which matches the problem's linear arrangement of children. We initialize the array with default values (1 candy for each child) because that's the minimum possible allocation. Then we use a loop to access each element sequentially. This works because arrays provide random access by index, and loops allow repetitive operations. The algorithm involves: 1) Creating an array of size n filled with 1s, 2) Using a for-loop with index variable i going from 0 to n-1, 3) Accessing array elements using the index. Time complexity for initialization is O(n), for looping is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the array. This connects to L4 as the structural foundation—without arrays and loops, we couldn't implement the comparison operations at scale. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges (e.g., going to n instead of n-1), confusing array indices (0-based vs 1-based), or incorrectly initializing arrays. The real pattern is fundamental to almost all array processing algorithms.

Pseudocode:
# Initialize array of size n with value 1
n = 5  # example size
candies = [1] * n

# Loop through array
for i in range(n):  # i goes 0, 1, 2, ..., n-1
    current = candies[i]
    # Do something with current element

# Alternative: while loop
i = 0
while i < n:
    current = candies[i]
    # Do something
    i = i + 1

examples.trace:
Initialize: candies = [1] * 3 = [1, 1, 1]
Loop with for i in range(3):
  i=0: access candies[0]=1
  i=1: access candies[1]=1
  i=2: access candies[2]=1
End loop

Initialize: candies = [1] * 0 = []
Loop: range(0) has no values, so loop body never executes

Initialize array of size 2, access both elements
Initialize array of size 3, try to access index 3
Initialize array of size 6, loop from 0 to 5

approaches.name: Two Pass Greedy O(n)
approaches.summary: Make two passes: left-to-right to ensure higher ratings than left neighbors get more candies, then right-to-left to handle higher ratings than right neighbors. Sum the results for the final distribution. This is the standard efficient solution.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Two Pass Greedy Candy Distribution
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two passes to distribute minimum candies while satisfying rating constraints
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires distributing candies to children with given ratings, where each child must get at least one candy, and children with higher ratings than their immediate neighbors must get more candies. The two-pass greedy approach solves this optimally in O(n) time and O(n) space. The intuition is that we can't determine a child's candy count by looking at both neighbors simultaneously, so we break it into two simpler problems: first ensure left neighbor constraints, then right neighbor constraints. In the first left-to-right pass, we give each child one more candy than their left neighbor if their rating is higher, otherwise just one candy. This handles all 'higher than left neighbor' constraints. In the second right-to-left pass, we adjust for 'higher than right neighbor' constraints by taking the maximum of the current candy count and (right neighbor's candies + 1) when the rating is higher. This ensures both constraints are satisfied while minimizing total candies. The algorithm: 1) Initialize candies array with 1s. 2) Left pass: for i from 1 to n-1, if ratings[i] > ratings[i-1], candies[i] = candies[i-1] + 1. 3) Right pass: for i from n-2 down to 0, if ratings[i] > ratings[i+1], candies[i] = max(candies[i], candies[i+1] + 1). 4) Sum all candies. Time: O(n) for two passes. Space: O(n) for candies array. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of comparing neighbors and updating candies. Common mistakes: forgetting to initialize with 1s, not using max() in right pass (which could reduce candies and violate left constraints), and off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: def minCandies(ratings): n=len(ratings); candies=[1]*n; for i in range(1,n): if ratings[i]>ratings[i-1]: candies[i]=candies[i-1]+1; for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): if ratings[i]>ratings[i+1]: candies[i]=max(candies[i], candies[i+1]+1); return sum(candies).

examples.trace: Ratings: [1,0,2]. Step 1: Initialize candies=[1,1,1]. Step 2: Left pass: i=1: ratings[1]=0 ≤ ratings[0]=1 → candies[1]=1. i=2: ratings[2]=2 > ratings[1]=0 → candies[2]=candies[1]+1=2. Candies after left pass: [1,1,2]. Step 3: Right pass: i=1: ratings[1]=0 ≤ ratings[2]=2 → no change. i=0: ratings[0]=1 > ratings[1]=0 → candies[0]=max(1, candies[1]+1=2)=2. Candies after right pass: [2,1,2]. Step 4: Sum=2+1+2=5.

Ratings: [1,2,2]. Step 1: Initialize candies=[1,1,1]. Step 2: Left pass: i=1: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[0]=1 → candies[1]=candies[0]+1=2. i=2: ratings[2]=2 ≤ ratings[1]=2 → candies[2]=1. Candies after left pass: [1,2,1]. Step 3: Right pass: i=1: ratings[1]=2 ≤ ratings[2]=2 → no change. i=0: ratings[0]=1 ≤ ratings[1]=2 → no change. Candies after right pass: [1,2,1]. Step 4: Sum=1+2+1=4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Neighbor Comparison and Candy Update
ladders.desc: The core operation of comparing ratings with neighbors and updating candy counts
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pass approach: comparing a child's rating with their neighbor and updating their candy count accordingly. The intuition is that we need to enforce a simple rule: if a child has a higher rating than a neighbor, they must get more candies. However, we can't enforce this for both neighbors at once, so we handle left and right neighbors separately in different passes. In each pass, we traverse the array and for each child, check if their rating is greater than the neighbor in the direction of traversal. If yes, we update their candy count to be at least one more than the neighbor's current candy count. The key insight is that in the left pass, we only consider left neighbors, so we can update greedily. In the right pass, we must use max() to preserve any higher candy counts from the left pass. The algorithm for one pass: given ratings array, candies array, and direction (left or right), iterate in that direction, compare current rating with neighbor, and update candies[current] = max(candies[current], candies[neighbor] + 1) if rating is higher. Time: O(n) for one pass. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called twice in the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of handling one constraint direction. Common mistakes: not using max() in the second pass (which can decrease candies and violate first pass constraints), incorrect neighbor indexing, and not handling equal ratings (no update needed). Real code pattern: def updatePass(ratings, candies, start, end, step, neighbor_offset): for i in range(start, end, step): if ratings[i] > ratings[i+neighbor_offset]: candies[i] = max(candies[i], candies[i+neighbor_offset] + 1).

examples.trace: Initial candies: [1,1,1]. Step 1: i=1: compare ratings[1]=3 > ratings[0]=1 → update candies[1] = max(1, candies[0]+1=2) = 2. Step 2: i=2: compare ratings[2]=2 ≤ ratings[1]=3 → no update. Final candies: [1,2,1].

Initial candies: [1,2,1]. Step 1: i=1: compare ratings[1]=3 > ratings[2]=2 → update candies[1] = max(2, candies[2]+1=2) = 2. Step 2: i=0: compare ratings[0]=1 ≤ ratings[1]=3 → no update. Final candies: [1,2,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling One Constraint Direction Greedily
ladders.desc: The technique of satisfying constraints in one direction using a single pass
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic of handling all constraints in one direction (either left or right) using a greedy single pass. The intuition is that constraints in one direction are simpler because they form a chain: if child A has higher rating than left neighbor B, A needs more candies than B. But B might have its own left neighbor constraint. By processing left-to-right, we can ensure each child gets enough candies relative to their left neighbor because we've already computed the left neighbor's candies. This creates a dependency chain that we resolve sequentially. The algorithm: initialize all candies to 1. Traverse from left to right. For each child i (starting from 1), if rating[i] > rating[i-1], set candies[i] = candies[i-1] + 1. This guarantees that every child gets at least one more candy than their left neighbor if their rating is higher. The greedy choice works because giving the minimum possible (just one more) doesn't affect future children negatively. Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for candies array. Connection to L1: This is a specific application of the neighbor comparison subroutine for left neighbors only. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of comparing adjacent elements. Common mistakes: starting the loop from 0 instead of 1 (no left neighbor for first child), not initializing candies to 1, and not handling equal ratings correctly (no update). Real code pattern: def leftPass(ratings): n=len(ratings); candies=[1]*n; for i in range(1,n): if ratings[i]>ratings[i-1]: candies[i]=candies[i-1]+1; return candies.

examples.trace: Ratings: [1,2,3,4,5]. Step 1: Initialize candies=[1,1,1,1,1]. Step 2: i=1: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[0]=1 → candies[1]=candies[0]+1=2. Step 3: i=2: ratings[2]=3 > ratings[1]=2 → candies[2]=candies[1]+1=3. Step 4: i=3: ratings[3]=4 > ratings[2]=3 → candies[3]=candies[2]+1=4. Step 5: i=4: ratings[4]=5 > ratings[3]=4 → candies[4]=candies[3]+1=5. Final candies: [1,2,3,4,5].

Ratings: [5,4,3,2,1]. Step 1: Initialize candies=[1,1,1,1,1]. Step 2: i=1: ratings[1]=4 ≤ ratings[0]=5 → no change. Step 3: i=2: ratings[2]=3 ≤ ratings[1]=4 → no change. Step 4: i=3: ratings[3]=2 ≤ ratings[2]=3 → no change. Step 5: i=4: ratings[4]=1 ≤ ratings[3]=2 → no change. Final candies: [1,1,1,1,1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Adjacent Array Elements
ladders.desc: The intermediate concept of comparing each element with its neighbor in an array
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing an array and comparing each element with its immediate neighbor (either left or right). This is fundamental to the candy problem because constraints are based on comparisons between adjacent children's ratings. The intuition is that many array problems require examining relationships between consecutive elements. Here, we need to know if a child's rating is higher than their neighbor's to determine candy distribution. The algorithm is simple: iterate through the array, and for each position i (except boundaries), compare array[i] with array[i-1] for left neighbor or array[i+1] for right neighbor. We can perform actions based on the comparison, such as updating another array. Time: O(n) for one pass. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L2: This is the basic operation used in the left or right pass to check constraints. Connection to L4: This builds on array traversal. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing leading to out-of-bounds errors, not handling the first or last element specially, and off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: def compareAdjacent(arr, direction='left'): n=len(arr); for i in range(1,n) if direction=='left' else range(n-1): if direction=='left': compare arr[i] and arr[i-1]; else: compare arr[i] and arr[i+1]; perform action based on comparison.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=1: compare arr[1]=3 with left neighbor arr[0]=5 → 3 < 5. Step 2: i=2: compare arr[2]=8 with left neighbor arr[1]=3 → 8 > 3. Step 3: i=3: compare arr[3]=2 with left neighbor arr[2]=8 → 2 < 8.

Step 1: i=0: compare arr[0]=1 with right neighbor arr[1]=4 → 1 < 4. Step 2: i=1: compare arr[1]=4 with right neighbor arr[2]=2 → 4 > 2. Step 3: i=2: compare arr[2]=2 with right neighbor arr[3]=5 → 2 < 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversing an Array with Index Updates
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of iterating through an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves looping through all elements of an array using an index variable. This is essential for the candy problem because we need to access each child's rating and update their candy count. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and processing them requires sequentially visiting each position. We can traverse forward (from first to last) or backward (from last to first), which corresponds to the two passes in the solution. The algorithm: use a for loop with index i going from 0 to n-1 for forward traversal, or from n-1 down to 0 for backward traversal. At each step, we can access array[i] and perform operations. Time: O(n) for one traversal. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L3: This traversal is used to compare adjacent elements by accessing array[i] and array[i-1] or array[i+1]. Connection to L5: This builds on the concept of arrays and loops. Common mistakes: incorrect loop boundaries causing off-by-one errors, modifying the array while iterating (if not careful), and using the wrong index for access. Real code pattern: def traverseArray(arr, direction='forward'): n=len(arr); if direction=='forward': for i in range(n): process arr[i]; else: for i in range(n-1, -1, -1): process arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0: access arr[0]=10. Step 2: i=1: access arr[1]=20. Step 3: i=2: access arr[2]=30.
Step 1: i=2: access arr[2]=7. Step 2: i=1: access arr[1]=3. Step 3: i=0: access arr[0]=5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Comparisons
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: understanding arrays and comparing numbers
ladders.explanation: This foundation level covers the basic concepts needed for the candy problem: arrays and number comparisons. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in order, accessible by index. In this problem, ratings are stored in an array where each index represents a child. Comparing numbers is fundamental because we need to determine if one child's rating is higher than another's. The intuition is that we can store data sequentially and make decisions based on relative values. For arrays, we need to know how to create them, access elements (e.g., ratings[0] for first child), and get their length. For comparisons, we use operators like >, <, = to compare two numbers. Time: O(1) for single access or comparison. Space: O(n) for storing the array. Connection to L4: This provides the data structure and operations used in array traversal. Common mistakes: confusing array indices (starting at 0), not handling empty arrays, and incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; first_element = arr[0]; if arr[0] > arr[1]: do something.

examples.input: Array: [5,3,8], access element at index 1 and compare with element at index 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[1] = 3. Step 2: Access arr[0] = 5. Step 3: Compare 3 and 5 using <: 3 < 5 is true.

examples.input: Array: [2,2,2], access all elements and check if they are equal
examples.output: All elements are 2, so all comparisons are equal
examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[0]=2, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=2. Step 2: Compare arr[0] and arr[1]: 2 == 2. Step 3: Compare arr[1] and arr[2]: 2 == 2.

approaches.name: Single Pass Peak-Valley O(n)
approaches.summary: Traverse the array once, tracking peaks and valleys in ratings to calculate candies on the fly using slope counting. This approach minimizes passes while maintaining correctness.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Candy Distribution with Single Pass Peak-Valley
ladders.desc: Complete solution to distribute minimum candies using single pass with slope counting
ladders.explanation: The candy distribution problem requires assigning at least 1 candy to each child, with higher-rated neighbors receiving more candies. The single pass peak-valley approach solves this in O(n) time and O(1) space by traversing the ratings array once while tracking slopes. The key insight is that we can count candies on the fly by treating the ratings as a sequence of ascending and descending slopes. When ratings increase, we increment a counter; when they decrease, we increment a different counter; when equal, we reset to 1. The total candies equals the sum of all slope counts plus adjustments for peaks where slopes meet. Intuitively, this works because each child's candy count depends only on their position within local slopes, not on global comparisons. We maintain two variables: 'up' for ascending sequences and 'down' for descending sequences. When we encounter a peak (transition from up to down), we add the maximum of up and down to account for the peak child receiving candies from both sides. The algorithm: Initialize total = 1, up = 1, down = 0, peak = 1. For i from 1 to n-1: if ratings[i] > ratings[i-1]: up += 1, down = 0, peak = up, total += up. elif ratings[i] < ratings[i-1]: down += 1, up = 1, total += down + (1 if down >= peak else 0). else: up = 1, down = 0, peak = 1, total += 1. Return total. Time: O(n) for single pass. Space: O(1) for counters. This feeds into the full problem by implementing the complete solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to reset counters on equal ratings, mishandling peak adjustments, off-by-one errors in indices. Real pattern: track slopes, adjust at transitions.

examples.trace: ratings = [1,0,2], n=3. Initialize total=1 (for index 0), up=1, down=0, peak=1. i=1: ratings[1]=0 < ratings[0]=1 → descending: down=1, up=1, total=1+down=2, peak remains 1 (down=1 >= peak=1? yes, but adjustment only if down>peak, so no extra). i=2: ratings[2]=2 > ratings[1]=0 → ascending: up=2, down=0, peak=up=2, total=2+up=4. End: total=4? Wait, check: child 0 gets 2 (from down sequence), child 1 gets 1, child 2 gets 2 → total=5. Let's trace carefully: Start: total=1 (candy for index 0). i=1: descending, down=1, total=1+1=2 (candy for index 1 is down=1). i=2: ascending, up=2, total=2+2=4 (candy for index 2 is up=2). But correct distribution: [2,1,2] = 5. Error: need to add peak adjustment. Actually, algorithm: total += down + (1 if down >= peak else 0). At i=1: down=1, peak=1, down>=peak → add 1? Let's recalc: Initialize total=1, up=1, down=0, peak=1. i=1: ratings[1]<ratings[0] → down=1, up=1, total=1+down+(1 if down>=peak else 0)=1+1+1=3 (since down=1>=peak=1). i=2: ratings[2]>ratings[1] → up=2, down=0, peak=up=2, total=3+up=5. Yes, total=5. Distribution: index0: from down? Actually, candy for index0 is determined by peak? Let's compute candies per child: child0: at start up=1 → candy=1, but after i=1 descending, child0 gets down+1? Better to derive: final candies: [2,1,2]. Trace matches total=5.

ratings = [1,2,2], n=3. Initialize total=1, up=1, down=0, peak=1. i=1: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[0]=1 → ascending: up=2, down=0, peak=up=2, total=1+up=3. i=2: ratings[2]=2 == ratings[1]=2 → equal: up=1, down=0, peak=1, total=3+1=4. Distribution: [1,2,1] = 4. Check: child0=1, child1=2, child2=1. Correct.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Slope Counting with Peak Adjustment
ladders.desc: Count candies along slopes and adjust at peaks where ascending meets descending
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core of the single pass approach: traversing the ratings array and counting candies based on local slopes. The intuition is that candy distribution follows natural 'slopes' in ratings: when ratings increase, candies increase; when decrease, candies increase in the opposite direction; when equal, reset to 1. The challenge is at peaks (local maxima) where an ascending slope meets a descending slope—the child at the peak must receive candies from both sides, so we take the maximum of the up and down counts. The algorithm: given ratings array, initialize total=1 (for first child), up=1, down=0, peak=1. Iterate i from 1 to n-1: if ratings[i] > ratings[i-1]: ascending slope: increment up, reset down=0, set peak=up, add up to total. elif ratings[i] < ratings[i-1]: descending slope: increment down, reset up=1, add down to total, and if down >= peak, add 1 extra (peak adjustment). else: equal ratings: reset up=1, down=0, peak=1, add 1 to total. Return total. Time: O(n) for the iteration. Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the main logic loop. Common mistakes: incorrect peak adjustment (e.g., adding when down > peak instead of >=), not resetting counters on equal ratings. Real pattern: use if-elif-else for three cases, update counters accordingly.

examples.trace: ratings = [1,2,1], n=3. Initialize total=1, up=1, down=0, peak=1. i=1: ratings[1]=2 > ratings[0]=1 → ascending: up=2, down=0, peak=2, total=1+2=3. i=2: ratings[2]=1 < ratings[1]=2 → descending: down=1, up=1, total=3+down=4, check down>=peak? down=1, peak=2 → false, no extra. Total=4. Distribution: [1,2,1] = 4.

ratings = [2,1,3], n=3. Initialize total=1, up=1, down=0, peak=1. i=1: ratings[1]=1 < ratings[0]=2 → descending: down=1, up=1, total=1+down=2, down>=peak? down=1>=peak=1 → true, add 1: total=3. i=2: ratings[2]=3 > ratings[1]=1 → ascending: up=2, down=0, peak=2, total=3+up=5. Total=5. Distribution: [2,1,2] = 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying and Handling Peaks and Valleys
ladders.desc: Detect transitions between ascending and descending sequences to determine candy counts
ladders.explanation: The core logic revolves around identifying peaks (local maxima) and valleys (local minima) in the ratings array to allocate candies efficiently. A peak occurs when an ascending sequence ends and a descending sequence begins (ratings[i-1] < ratings[i] > ratings[i+1]), while a valley is the opposite. In candy distribution, children at peaks need candies from both sides, so their count is the maximum of the left ascending length and right descending length. The single pass approach simplifies this by tracking slopes: we maintain 'up' count for current ascending length and 'down' count for current descending length. When we transition from ascending to descending (i.e., hit a peak), we store the 'up' value as 'peak'. During the descending slope, if 'down' reaches or exceeds 'peak', we add an extra candy to account for the peak child needing more from the descending side. This ensures the peak child gets max(up, down) candies. The intuition: as we traverse, we're essentially building slopes and merging them at peaks without needing to look ahead. Time: O(n) for checking each element. Space: O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the transition handling. Common mistakes: misidentifying peaks when ratings are equal, not handling plateaus correctly. Real pattern: compare current and previous ratings to detect slope changes.

examples.trace: Identify peaks and valleys: ratings = [1,2,3,1]. Peaks: at index 2 (3 > 2 and 3 > 1). Ascending slope from index 0 to 2: length 3. Descending slope from index 2 to 3: length 2. Peak child gets max(3,2)=3 candies. Valley at index 0 and 3 get 1 each. Distribution: [1,2,3,1] = 7. Using slope counting: up counts: at index2, up=3; down from index2: down=2; peak=3; down<peak, so no extra. Total candies: sum of slopes: ascending gives 1+2+3=6, descending gives +1? Let's compute via algorithm: total=1, up=1, down=0, peak=1. i=1: asc, up=2, total=3. i=2: asc, up=3, total=6. i=3: desc, down=1, total=7, down<peak? down=1<peak=3, no extra. Total=7.

ratings = [3,2,1,2]. Peaks: at index 0? 3 > 2 (but no left), so not a peak in middle. Valley at index 2 (1 < 2 and 1 < 2). Ascending slope from index 2 to 3: length 2. Descending slope from index 0 to 2: length 3. Peak at index 0? Actually, index0 is start of descending, so treat as peak? In algorithm: total=1, up=1, down=0, peak=1. i=1: desc, down=1, total=2, down>=peak? 1>=1 → add1: total=3. i=2: desc, down=2, total=5, down>=peak? 2>=1 → add1: total=6. i=3: asc, up=2, total=8? Wait, error. Let's trace stepwise: i=1: ratings[1]=2 < ratings[0]=3 → desc: down=1, up=1, total=1+1+(1 if down>=peak)=1+1+1=3. i=2: ratings[2]=1 < ratings[1]=2 → desc: down=2, up=1, total=3+2+(1 if down>=peak)=3+2+1=6. i=3: ratings[3]=2 > ratings[2]=1 → asc: up=2, down=0, peak=up=2, total=6+2=8. But distribution should be [3,2,1,2] = 7? Actually, correct: [3,2,1,2] candies: child0=3, child1=2, child2=1, child3=2 → total=8. My mistake: expected 7 is wrong. Check: ratings [3,2,1,2]: minimum candies: child2=1, child3=2, child1 must be >child2 so at least 2, child0 must be >child1 so at least 3 → [3,2,1,2]=8. So output 8. Let's correct: expected 8. Trace gives total=8.

ladders.title: Building Block — Counting Lengths of Ascending and Descending Sequences
ladders.desc: Compute the length of consecutive increasing or decreasing subsequences in an array
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing an array and counting how many consecutive elements are strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. For candy distribution, these lengths determine how many candies to assign along slopes. The intuition: if ratings increase over k consecutive children, the first gets 1 candy, second gets 2, ..., k-th gets k candies, so total candies for that slope is the sum of 1 to k = k(k+1)/2. Similarly for decreasing slopes, but counted backwards. The algorithm: given array arr, initialize up=1, down=1, and lists to store lengths. For i from 1 to n-1: if arr[i] > arr[i-1]: increment up, reset down=1. elif arr[i] < arr[i-1]: increment down, reset up=1. else: reset both to 1. Record up and down at each step or at slope ends. Time: O(n). Space: O(1) if not storing, or O(n) if storing lengths. This feeds into L2 by providing the slope lengths needed for peak handling. Common mistakes: not resetting counters on equal elements, off-by-one in length counting. Real pattern: use if-else to update counters based on comparisons.

examples.trace: arr = [1,2,3,2,1], n=5. Initialize up=1, down=1. i=1: 2>1 → up=2, down=1. i=2: 3>2 → up=3, down=1. i=3: 2<3 → down=2, up=1. i=4: 1<2 → down=3, up=1. So at each index, up counts: index0:1, index1:2, index2:3, index3:1, index4:1. Down counts: index0:1, index1:1, index2:1, index3:2, index4:3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Adjacent Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and compare each element with its neighbor
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves looping through an array and performing comparisons between consecutive elements. It's fundamental for detecting trends like increasing or decreasing sequences. The intuition: by comparing arr[i] with arr[i-1], we can determine if the sequence is going up, down, or staying flat. This is done using relational operators: >, <, ==. The algorithm: given array arr of length n, for i from 1 to n-1: compare arr[i] and arr[i-1]. Based on the result, take action (e.g., increment counters). Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism to identify slopes. Common mistakes: accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., i=0 for arr[i-1]), misunderstanding comparison results. Real pattern: for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] > arr[i-1]: ...

examples.trace: arr = [1,3,2], n=3. i=1: compare arr[1]=3 and arr[0]=1 → 3 > 1, so increasing. i=2: compare arr[2]=2 and arr[1]=3 → 2 < 3, so decreasing.
arr = [5,5,4], n=3. i=1: compare 5 and 5 → equal. i=2: compare 4 and 5 → 4 < 5, decreasing.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterating Through an Array with a Loop
ladders.desc: Basic loop to access each element in an array sequentially
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves using a loop to traverse an array from start to end. An array is a collection of elements indexed from 0 to n-1. Iteration allows us to examine or modify each element. The intuition: many algorithms require processing all elements, and loops provide a systematic way to do so. The algorithm: given array arr of length n, use a for loop: for i in range(n): access arr[i]. Alternatively, to compare with next element, loop from 0 to n-2 or 1 to n-1. Time: O(n) for visiting n elements. Space: O(1) if not using extra storage. This feeds into L4 by providing the loop structure for comparisons. Common mistakes: incorrect range bounds leading to index errors, misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): ...
